skill.name: neon-postgres
skill.description: Guidance for building serverless applications with Neon PostgreSQL, including connection methods, pooling, branching, and framework integration.
skill.summary: Build Neon PostgreSQL integrations for serverless and full-stack applications.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, and credential patterns, but review found they are mainly Markdown examples and one reusable database template. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration, prompt injection, hidden execution, or obfuscation. The skill remains medium risk because it teaches real Neon CLI operations, package installation, environment secret handling, and live database connections.

medium_findings.title: Operational CLI Commands Affect Remote Neon Projects
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Neon CLI commands for authentication, branch creation, connection-string retrieval, and branch deletion. These are legitimate for this domain, but they can change remote database resources if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly visible and semantically tied to Neon project operations. Confidence is high for operational risk, but there is no evidence that the skill executes them automatically.

medium_findings.title: Environment Secrets Required for Database Connections
medium_findings.description: The skill and template rely on DATABASE_URL and GitHub secrets for real database access. This is expected for a Neon PostgreSQL skill, but users must avoid exposing connection strings in prompts, logs, or generated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The use of database URLs and CI secrets is directly present in examples and template code. The pattern is legitimate, so the risk is handling exposure rather than confirmed theft.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick and shell substitution detections correspond to Markdown code fences and TypeScript tagged SQL templates, not hidden local command execution in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows the matches are fenced examples or commented usage samples. No executable script file or automatic command runner was found in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined command examples, network database guidance, and credential variables into a critical heuristic. Manual review found no obfuscation, exfiltration endpoint, or instruction to bypass security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The combined categories are real, but they support the advertised database workflow. Prompt-injection keyword search and line review found no malicious control text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Write and Delete Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes INSERT, DELETE, migration, and branch deletion examples. These are normal database administration patterns but should be reviewed before use against production data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The potentially destructive operations are explicit in examples. They are not malicious by themselves, but they create real operational risk when copied into production workflows.

content.value_statement: Serverless apps need reliable PostgreSQL access without connection exhaustion. This skill provides Neon patterns for connections, pooling, branching, and framework setup.
actual_capabilities: Explains Neon HTTP and WebSocket connection methods for serverless applications.
Provides installation and connection string examples for Neon projects.
Shows Drizzle ORM and Next.js integration patterns.
Documents Neon CLI workflows for branches and preview environments.
Includes a TypeScript database connection template for Neon serverless usage.
limitations: It does not create a Neon project or provision credentials automatically.
It assumes the user already has a valid Neon database connection string.
Several linked guides are referenced but are not included in the scanned package.
It provides examples, not a complete migration or schema management system.

use_cases.title: Set Up Serverless Database Access
use_cases.description: Choose the Neon HTTP driver or WebSocket pool and wire it into a serverless application.

use_cases.title: Create Preview Database Branches
use_cases.description: Plan Neon branch creation and cleanup for pull request preview environments.

use_cases.title: Add Typed Database Queries
use_cases.description: Use the provided Drizzle and TypeScript patterns to connect application code to Neon PostgreSQL.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Connection Method
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between Neon HTTP and WebSocket pooling for my serverless app. Explain the tradeoffs and recommended default.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Next.js Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a step-by-step plan to connect a Next.js app to Neon PostgreSQL using environment variables and safe query patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Review Database Branch Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my pull request preview database workflow for Neon branches. Identify missing cleanup, secret handling, and migration steps.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Production Connections
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Neon PostgreSQL connection strategy for production serverless traffic. Recommend pooling, timeouts, and cold-start handling changes.

output_examples.input: I need a Neon database setup for a serverless API.
output_examples.output: A short implementation plan that recommends the HTTP driver, names required environment variables, and lists safe query practices.

output_examples.input: Our preview deployments need isolated data.
output_examples.output: A branch workflow covering creation, migration, connection string use, and cleanup after pull request closure.

output_examples.input: My serverless app opens too many database connections.
output_examples.output: A connection strategy review that compares HTTP queries, WebSocket pooling, timeouts, and production safeguards.

best_practices: Use parameterized queries and avoid string-built SQL statements.
Store database URLs in environment variables or secret managers.
Use Neon branches for previews and delete unused branches promptly.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real database passwords into prompts or shared tickets.
Do not use long-lived connection pools in short serverless functions without review.
Do not run branch deletion or migration commands against production by mistake.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to Neon automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates. The user must provide credentials and run any commands.

faq.question: Which connection method should serverless apps use?
faq.answer: The skill recommends the Neon HTTP driver for most edge and serverless one-shot query workloads.

faq.question: Can it help with Drizzle ORM?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes Drizzle examples for Neon HTTP and WebSocket connection patterns.

faq.question: Does it include migration tooling?
faq.answer: It references migration commands, but it does not include a full migration system or generated schemas.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it handles database secrets and operational CLI workflows.

faq.question: What should users protect most carefully?
faq.answer: Users should protect DATABASE_URL values, Neon API keys, and production branch operations.
